refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output (energy_per_ton) to support evaluation against a customer-defined energy efficiency target.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the capability to measure/report energy efficiency metrics, and (2) the achievement of a performance target. The refined FR focuses on the functional requirement—enabling visibility and traceability of energy efficiency data—which is solution-neutral and within system control. Achievement of the target (E_target ≤ energy_per_ton) is a performance acceptance criterion, not a functional requirement. This separation clarifies that the system must provide the measurement infrastructure; actual target achievement depends on design choices (DP/IM) and operational conditions.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verify that the NGHE solution collects, calculates, and reports energy_per_ton metrics (energy_used / ton_moved) with defined accuracy and temporal granularity over the agreed evaluation period, independent of whether the reported value meets the customer's target threshold.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall measure total energy consumed (kWh or equivalent) during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall measure or infer total work output (tons moved or equivalent unit) during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall calculate energy_per_ton as the ratio of energy consumed to work output.
FR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall record and report energy_per_ton metrics with sufficient temporal and spatial resolution to support performance evaluation.

candidate_dps: DP1: Instrument energy input (fuel/electricity) at source; instrument work output (tonnage moved) at point of delivery; compute ratio offline post-evaluation period; report against target.
DP2: Embed real-time energy and tonnage sensors; stream data to onboard compute module; calculate energy_per_ton continuously; trigger alerts if threshold breached; log for post-evaluation reporting.

DP3: Integrate energy metering at power distribution node; integrate tonnage counting at load transfer point; synchronize timestamps; batch-calculate energy_per_ton at shift/day boundaries; store in local database; export for compliance review.

DP4: Establish energy consumption baseline via calibrated measurement during controlled test cycle; apply correction factors for operational variance; estimate energy_per_ton from proxy metrics (fuel consumption rate, load mass, distance); validate against periodic spot checks.

DP5: Decompose into independent measurement subsystems: (a) energy accounting subsystem measures cumulative input; (b) work accounting subsystem measures cumulative output; (c) aggregation subsystem computes ratio and compares to target; each subsystem operates autonomously with defined handoff interfaces.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP5: Decompose into independent measurement subsystems: (a) energy accounting subsystem measures cumulative input; (b) work accounting subsystem measures cumulative output; (c) aggregation subsystem computes ratio and compares to target; each subsystem operates autonomously with defined handoff interfaces.

potential_sources: Timestamp synchronization between energy and work subsystems—mitigated by explicit interface contract requiring synchronized clock or event-driven handoff.
Data format coupling—mitigated by defining neutral schema (cumulative energy in joules, cumulative tonnage in metric tons, evaluation period boundaries).
Dependency on shared infrastructure (power, network)—acknowledged as operational constraint, not architectural coupling; each subsystem can function independently if infrastructure is available.

DP1, DP2, DP4 exhibit tighter coupling: DP1 couples measurement timing to post-evaluation analysis (no real-time feedback); DP2 couples sensor selection, compute platform, and alerting logic; DP4 couples baseline calibration to operational variance model, reducing independence.
coupling_check.notes: DP5 achieves lowest architectural coupling by separating concerns: energy measurement is independent of tonnage measurement; aggregation logic is independent of sensor technology. Allows substitution of measurement methods without redesign of ratio calculation. Supports phased deployment and independent testing.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Energy input and work output can be measured independently with acceptable accuracy and latency. RISK: If measurement uncertainty is high (>5%), ratio may not reliably discriminate against target; recommend uncertainty budget analysis.

ASSUMPTION: Evaluation period is well-defined and communicated to all stakeholders. RISK: Ambiguity in period boundaries (e.g., partial shifts, maintenance windows) could invalidate comparison; recommend explicit inclusion in acceptance criteria.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan permits installation of measurement instrumentation without introducing new hazards. RISK: Sensor placement, wiring, or data collection may conflict with safety protocols; recommend early coordination with safety officer.

ASSUMPTION: Energy and work data can be synchronized to sufficient precision (e.g., within 1 minute) to avoid systematic bias. RISK: Clock drift or asynchronous logging could introduce systematic error; recommend periodic calibration and audit.

ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined energy efficiency target E_target is technically achievable with the NGHE solution under normal operating conditions. RISK: If target is unrealistic, system will fail acceptance regardless of design quality; recommend feasibility study before commitment.

ASSUMPTION: Operational policies (e.g., load profiles, duty cycles, environmental conditions) remain stable during evaluation period. RISK: Significant variance in operations could confound energy_per_ton measurement; recommend baseline characterization and variance documentation.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR should be refined to explicitly state: (1) Required measurement accuracy and uncertainty tolerance (e.g., ±3% for energy, ±2% for tonnage); (2) Synchronization tolerance between energy and work measurements (e.g., ±5 minutes); (3) Definition of evaluation period boundaries and handling of partial periods; (4) Scope of operational conditions (load profiles, ambient, duty cycles) over which energy_per_ton must be valid; (5) Frequency and method of reporting (real-time dashboard, daily summary, post-period audit). This will enable DP validation and IM design to proceed with clear acceptance criteria. Additionally, confirm that E_target has been validated as achievable through preliminary testing or engineering analysis.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates all three independent measurement subsystems (energy accounting, work accounting, aggregation) and their autonomous operation with defined handoff interfaces. This is the only module broad enough to contain the complete decomposed architecture without fragmentation.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives raw energy input measurements and work output measurements from Vehicle subsystems; feeds data into energy and work accounting subsystems

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Outputs aggregation results (efficiency ratio, target comparison, control decisions) to Vehicle or Teleoperation for actuation/response

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emits alerts if efficiency ratio deviates critically from target, triggering Safety Services intervention

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent containment: all three subsystems (energy, work, aggregation) are logically cohesive measurement and control functions. Minimal diffusion: only boundary crossings are standardized telemetry input and tasking/safety output. This module is purpose-built for energy-efficiency measurement and optimization.

Poor containment: Vehicle is a physical platform, not a measurement/control logic container. Energy and work accounting logic would be scattered across motor controllers, sensors, and actuators. Aggregation logic has no natural home. High diffusion: measurement subsystems would leak into multiple vehicle subsystems (propulsion, sensing, control).

Poor containment: Teleoperation is a human-machine interface layer, not a measurement subsystem container. Energy/work accounting and aggregation are autonomous functions, not operator-driven. High diffusion: would require duplicating measurement logic in both autonomous and teleoperated modes.

Poor containment: Safety Services is a reactive alert/intervention module, not a measurement subsystem. Energy accounting, work accounting, and aggregation are primary measurement functions, not safety-specific. High diffusion: measurement logic would be entangled with safety logic, violating separation of concerns.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: DP5 decomposes the efficiency measurement problem into three autonomous subsystems with defined handoff interfaces. This is a measurement and control architecture, not a physical platform, teleoperation interface, or safety function. NGHE Solution is the only module whose purpose is to measure, aggregate, and optimize energy efficiency. All three subsystems (energy accounting, work accounting, aggregation) are cohesive within this boundary, and external interfaces (Telemetry in, Tasking/SafetyAlert out) are clean and minimal.
containment_risks: Risk: If Vehicle subsystems do not expose standardized telemetry interfaces (energy input, work output), the energy and work accounting subsystems may need to reach into Vehicle internals, increasing coupling.
Risk: If Tasking interface is not well-defined, aggregation subsystem outputs may diffuse into multiple Vehicle control paths, reducing autonomy.
Risk: If SafetyAlert is not a first-class interface, safety-critical efficiency violations may require ad-hoc communication paths outside the defined handoff architecture.
Risk: Autonomous operation of three subsystems requires careful synchronization; if handoff interfaces lack timing/ordering semantics, subsystems may operate on stale or inconsistent data.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP5 correctly decomposes the FR into three autonomous, logically independent measurement subsystems (energy accounting, work accounting, aggregation) with defined handoff interfaces. The decomposition directly addresses the FR requirement to measure energy_per_ton and compare against E_target. Each subsystem has a clear, singular responsibility, and the aggregation subsystem explicitly performs the ratio computation and target comparison mandated by the FR. No functional gaps identified.

diffusion_risks: CRITICAL: Vehicle subsystem lacks standardized telemetry interface contract. If energy input and work output measurements are not formally exposed as first-class interfaces, energy and work accounting subsystems will be forced to couple directly to motor controllers, sensors, and actuators, causing high diffusion across Vehicle internals.

HIGH: Tasking interface semantics undefined. If aggregation subsystem outputs (efficiency ratio, target comparison, control decisions) lack formal interface specification, downstream consumers (Vehicle control, Teleoperation) may implement ad-hoc polling or event handling, fragmenting the aggregation logic across multiple modules.

HIGH: SafetyAlert interface lacks timing and priority semantics. If safety-critical efficiency violations do not trigger deterministic, time-bounded alerts through a dedicated interface, safety-critical paths may bypass the defined handoff architecture, creating hidden coupling to Safety Services.

MEDIUM: Synchronization semantics between three autonomous subsystems not specified. If energy accounting, work accounting, and aggregation operate without explicit timing constraints or data consistency guarantees, subsystems may compute ratios on stale or misaligned data, violating the acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target over evaluation period).

MEDIUM: No explicit module boundary for 'NGHE Solution' in the provided system architecture. If NGHE Solution is not instantiated as a distinct, owned module (e.g., a software service, firmware partition, or hardware subsystem), the three measurement subsystems risk being scattered across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services during implementation.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution achieves 95% containment and 5% diffusion for the three measurement subsystems. This is excellent logical cohesion: energy accounting, work accounting, and aggregation are all measurement and control functions with no natural home outside NGHE Solution. External interfaces (Telemetry in, Tasking/SafetyAlert out) are clean and minimal. However, containment is contingent on Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services providing well-defined, standardized interfaces. If these upstream/downstream modules do not honor the handoff contract, diffusion will increase significantly. Recommend formalizing interface specifications (data schemas, timing, priority) before implementation.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: FEASIBLE with conditions. The acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target over evaluation period) is directly measurable if: (1) energy accounting subsystem has access to cumulative energy input via Telemetry interface; (2) work accounting subsystem has access to cumulative work output (ton_moved) via Telemetry interface; (3) aggregation subsystem computes the ratio and logs results with timestamps; (4) evaluation period is clearly defined and synchronized across all three subsystems. Recommend instrumenting each subsystem with audit logging to trace energy_per_ton computation and enable post-hoc verification against E_target.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: CLEAR. The FR is fully allocated to NGHE Solution via DP5. Energy accounting subsystem owns cumulative energy measurement; work accounting subsystem owns cumulative work measurement; aggregation subsystem owns ratio computation and target comparison. No functional gaps or orphaned requirements. However, allocation clarity depends on Vehicle and Teleoperation providing the required telemetry data and accepting tasking/safety outputs. Recommend creating a formal allocation matrix (FR -> DP -> IM) and a traceability document linking each subsystem to specific acceptance criteria.

recommended_revisions: REVISION 1 (CRITICAL): Formalize Telemetry interface contract. Define data schemas, units, sampling rates, and latency bounds for energy input and work output measurements. Specify whether Vehicle subsystems push telemetry or NGHE Solution pulls; define buffering and synchronization semantics to ensure energy and work accounting subsystems operate on aligned data.

REVISION 2 (CRITICAL): Formalize Tasking interface contract. Define output data schema (efficiency ratio, target comparison result, control decision), timing semantics (real-time vs. batch), and priority levels. Specify how Vehicle and Teleoperation consume tasking outputs and what happens if they do not acknowledge or act on them.

REVISION 3 (CRITICAL): Formalize SafetyAlert interface contract. Define alert severity levels, trigger conditions (e.g., efficiency ratio exceeds E_target by X%), latency bounds, and acknowledgment requirements. Specify whether SafetyAlert is synchronous (blocking) or asynchronous (non-blocking) and how Safety Services prioritizes efficiency alerts relative to other safety events.

REVISION 4 (HIGH): Define synchronization and consistency semantics for the three autonomous subsystems. Specify: (a) whether energy and work accounting operate on the same time window or can drift; (b) how aggregation subsystem handles partial data (e.g., energy measured but work not yet available); (c) whether ratio computation is real-time, periodic, or event-driven; (d) how stale data is detected and handled.

REVISION 5 (HIGH): Instantiate NGHE Solution as a distinct, owned module in the system architecture. Assign ownership, define module boundaries (e.g., software service, firmware partition, hardware subsystem), and specify how it integrates with Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. Create a module interface control document (ICD) for each external interface.

REVISION 6 (MEDIUM): Add acceptance criteria for interface compliance. For example: 'Telemetry interface shall deliver energy and work measurements with <100ms latency and <1% data loss over the evaluation period.' 'Tasking interface shall deliver aggregation results within 1 second of computation.' 'SafetyAlert shall trigger within 5 seconds of efficiency violation detection.'

REVISION 7 (MEDIUM): Define evaluation period semantics. Specify whether energy_per_ton is computed over a fixed time window (e.g., 1 hour), a fixed work volume (e.g., 1 ton), or a continuous rolling average. Specify how partial periods are handled (e.g., if evaluation period ends mid-operation).

REVISION 8 (MEDIUM): Add traceability from DP5 subsystems to acceptance criteria. Create a matrix showing which subsystem (energy accounting, work accounting, aggregation) is responsible for each aspect of the acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target). Include audit logging and verification procedures.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high logical coherence and clear functional allocation. However, the DP-IM gate identifies CRITICAL and HIGH diffusion risks centered on undefined interface contracts (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert) and missing synchronization semantics. These risks directly threaten the acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) and safety criticality. Rather than revise at the current level (which would delay decomposition), the architecture is sound enough to proceed to the next level of sub-FRs for the three autonomous subsystems (energy accounting, work accounting, aggregation). At the next level, each subsystem's sub-FRs will explicitly address interface contracts, data consistency, and timing semantics. This approach allows parallel refinement of subsystem logic while capturing interface requirements as derived FRs. Revisions 1-5 (interface formalization and module instantiation) are prerequisites for implementation but do not block logical decomposition.
next_level_sub_frs: FR5.1: Measure cumulative energy input from Vehicle propulsion subsystem and expose via standardized Telemetry interface with defined schema, sampling rate, latency bounds, and data loss tolerance.

FR5.2: Measure cumulative work output (ton_moved) from Vehicle load subsystem and expose via standardized Telemetry interface with defined schema, sampling rate, latency bounds, and data loss tolerance.

FR5.3: Compute energy_per_ton ratio from aligned energy and work measurements, handle partial/stale data, and deliver results via standardized Tasking interface with defined output schema, timing semantics (real-time vs. batch), and priority levels.

FR5.4: Compare computed energy_per_ton against E_target acceptance criterion and trigger SafetyAlert via standardized interface with defined severity levels, trigger conditions, latency bounds, and acknowledgment requirements.

FR5.5: Synchronize energy accounting, work accounting, and aggregation subsystems to ensure data consistency, prevent ratio computation on misaligned measurements, and define handling of partial data and time window semantics.

FR5.6: Instantiate NGHE Solution as a distinct, owned module (software service, firmware partition, or hardware subsystem) with clear boundaries, ownership, and integration points with Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services.

revise.why: DP5 is logically sound but lacks explicit interface specifications. Recommend augmenting DP5 with a note that each of the three subsystems (energy accounting, work accounting, aggregation) must expose its outputs through formally specified interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert) with defined data schemas, timing semantics, and priority levels. This clarification will guide the next level of decomposition and ensure that interface contracts are treated as first-class design parameters, not afterthoughts. Additionally, recommend explicitly stating that NGHE Solution is a distinct, owned module with defined boundaries and integration contracts with upstream (Vehicle) and downstream (Teleoperation, Safety Services) modules.

assumptions: Vehicle subsystem will provide standardized Telemetry interface exposing energy input and work output measurements with defined schema and latency bounds.
Teleoperation and Safety Services will consume Tasking and SafetyAlert outputs from NGHE Solution and honor the defined interface contracts.
Energy accounting and work accounting subsystems can operate autonomously and synchronize via explicit timing/consistency semantics (not implicit coupling).
Evaluation period for energy_per_ton ≤ E_target is clearly defined (fixed time window, fixed work volume, or rolling average) and synchronized across all three subsystems.
NGHE Solution will be instantiated as a distinct, owned module with clear boundaries and integration points, not scattered across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services.
open_risks: CRITICAL: Telemetry interface contract undefined. If Vehicle does not expose energy and work measurements via a standardized, first-class interface, energy and work accounting subsystems will couple directly to motor controllers and sensors, causing high diffusion and maintenance burden.

CRITICAL: Tasking interface contract undefined. If aggregation subsystem outputs lack formal specification, downstream consumers may implement ad-hoc polling or event handling, fragmenting efficiency logic across multiple modules.

CRITICAL: SafetyAlert interface contract undefined. If safety-critical efficiency violations do not trigger deterministic, time-bounded alerts, safety-critical paths may bypass the defined architecture, creating hidden coupling to Safety Services.

HIGH: Synchronization semantics between three autonomous subsystems not specified. If energy accounting, work accounting, and aggregation operate without explicit timing constraints or data consistency guarantees, subsystems may compute ratios on stale or misaligned data, violating acceptance criterion.

HIGH: NGHE Solution module boundary not instantiated in system architecture. If NGHE Solution is not assigned as a distinct, owned module, the three measurement subsystems risk being scattered across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services during implementation.

MEDIUM: Evaluation period semantics undefined. If energy_per_ton is computed over a fixed time window, fixed work volume, or rolling average without clear definition, subsystems may interpret the acceptance criterion differently, leading to verification failures.

MEDIUM: Audit logging and traceability not specified. If each subsystem does not log energy_per_ton computation with timestamps and intermediate values, post-hoc verification against E_target will be difficult or impossible.
evidence_needed: Formal Telemetry interface specification (data schema, units, sampling rates, latency bounds, data loss tolerance, push vs. pull semantics).
Formal Tasking interface specification (output data schema, timing semantics, priority levels, acknowledgment requirements).
Formal SafetyAlert interface specification (severity levels, trigger conditions, latency bounds, acknowledgment requirements, synchronous vs. asynchronous semantics).
Synchronization and consistency semantics document (time window alignment, partial data handling, stale data detection, ratio computation frequency).
Module instantiation plan for NGHE Solution (ownership, boundaries, integration contracts with Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services).
Evaluation period definition (fixed time window, fixed work volume, rolling average, partial period handling).
Acceptance criteria for interface compliance (latency bounds, data loss tolerance, computation frequency, alert response time).
Traceability matrix linking DP5 subsystems to acceptance criteria and audit logging procedures.
